text: In every system of morality, which I have hitherto met with, I have always remarked, that the author proceeds for some time in the ordinary way of reasoning, and establishes the being of a God, or makes observations concerning human affairs; when of a sudden I am surprized to find, that instead of the usual copulations of propositions, is, and is not, I meet with no proposition that is not connected with an ought, or an ought not.
author: David Hume
source: Project Gutenberg eBook of A Treatise of Human Nature (ebook #4705)
out_pulls_note: Out-pulls the gloss decisively: this is the literal source of 'Hume's guillotine' - watching every moralist slide from 'is' to 'ought' without warrant - far richer than the abstract gloss.
search_notes: Found in Gutenberg #4705 (Hume, Treatise of Human Nature), Book III 'Of Morals,' Part I, Sect. I 'Moral distinctions not deriv'd from reason' - the famous is/ought paragraph. Verbatim contiguous span, byte-confirmed; kept Hume's spelling 'surprized'. 78 words. Truncated before 'This change is imperceptible...' to stay under cap and end cleanly. No verdict words (avoided 'prove/refute' etc.). High confidence.
